  • Security policies — MAC spoofing prevention, promiscuous mode control.
  • Traffic shaping and QoS — rate-limit VMs or guarantee bandwidth.
  • Link aggregation — bond multiple physical NICs for uplink redundancy and bandwidth.

Major vSwitch implementations:

  • VMware vSwitch / vDS — standard vSwitch per host, or vSphere Distributed Switch (vDS) centralized across cluster.
  • Hyper-V Virtual Switch — Microsoft's equivalent, with three types (external, internal, private).
  • Open vSwitch (OVS) — open-source, used with KVM and OpenStack. Supports OpenFlow and VXLAN for SDN.
  • Linux bridge — simple Linux-native bridge, often used with KVM in smaller deployments.

Network engineer's view: a vSwitch is an extension of the wired network. VLANs defined in the vSwitch must match VLANs configured on the upstream physical switch. A trunk port on the physical switch carries the VLANs to the ESXi/Hyper-V host, and the vSwitch demultiplexes them to the appropriate port groups.

hack.memory: 'vSwitch = switch in software.' Same concepts as hardware switch (MAC table, VLANs, trunking) but runs inside the hypervisor. VMs plug into it via vNICs.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: in VMware Workstation (Type 2 but same concept), View > Virtual Network Editor. You'll see VMnet0 (bridged), VMnet1 (host-only), VMnet8 (NAT) — these are vSwitches. On ESXi, create a vSwitch and assign port groups to VLAN IDs. Trunk the uplink to the physical switch.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Moderate exam weight — know vSwitch exists, supports VLANs, connects VMs to the wired network.

1.11.b.info:
A Type 2 hypervisor, also called a hosted hypervisor, runs as an application on top of an existing host operating system. The host OS (Windows, macOS, or Linux) owns the hardware; the Type 2 hypervisor is just another program running inside it. VMs created by a Type 2 hypervisor are two layers removed from hardware: hardware → host OS → hypervisor → guest VM.

This extra layer adds overhead. Every privileged instruction from the guest must pass through the hypervisor AND the host OS before reaching hardware. Performance overhead is typically 20-30% compared to a bare-metal Type 1 hypervisor. Memory usage is also higher because the host OS itself consumes RAM — a 16 GB laptop running Windows 11 has maybe 12 GB available for VMs after Windows takes its cut.

Primary use case: development, testing, and learning. Type 2 is what network engineers use on their laptops to run CCNA labs (GNS3, EVE-NG, Packet Tracer images), developers use to run different OS versions for testing, and security researchers use to run malware in isolated sandboxes. Production data centers don't use Type 2 because the performance penalty is unacceptable at scale.

Why Type 2 exists despite the overhead:

  • Easy install — just run the installer on your existing OS. No reboots, no repartitioning, no dedicated hardware.
  • Portability — your VM files can move between Windows, macOS, and Linux hosts.
  • Coexistence with regular OS usage — your laptop can run your normal apps AND host VMs simultaneously.
  • Low cost — most Type 2 hypervisors are free (VirtualBox) or bundled with hardware (Parallels on Mac).

Dependency on host OS: if the host OS crashes or reboots, all VMs die with it. Type 1 hypervisors don't have this dependency — they ARE the OS.

hack.memory: 'Type 2 = #2 on an OS.' Two layers between VMs and hardware (host OS + hypervisor). Easy to install, slower to run. Perfect for dev/test/labs, wrong for production.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: if you've ever used VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, or Parallels on your Mac/PC — you've used a Type 2 hypervisor. Compare VM performance to a known bare-metal install of the same OS; you'll feel the overhead.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: Type 2 = hosted on an OS, used for dev/test, not production.

1.11.b.info:
Oracle VirtualBox is the most popular free Type 2 hypervisor. Originally developed by Innotek, acquired by Sun Microsystems (2008), and now owned by Oracle, VirtualBox runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Solaris as hosts and supports a wide range of guest OSes.

Why VirtualBox is popular:

  • Free for personal and some commercial use — the base hypervisor is free; the Extension Pack (adds USB 2.0/3.0, RDP, disk encryption) is free for personal/education/eval but requires a license for commercial deployment.
  • Cross-platform — same UI and VM files work across Windows, macOS, and Linux. You can move a VM between host OSes without modification.
  • Open source (mostly) — the base hypervisor is GPL-licensed.
  • Wide guest OS support — runs Windows, Linux, BSD, Solaris, and more as guests.

Key features:

  • Snapshots — save VM state and revert later, essential for testing.
  • Shared folders — mount host folders inside the guest VM.
  • Seamless mode — guest windows integrate into host desktop (Windows XP on Mac, for example).
  • Four network modes — NAT, bridged, host-only, internal. NAT is the default and works out of the box.
  • VirtualBox Guest Additions — drivers installed in the guest for better integration (shared clipboard, display resolution, mouse pointer).

Limitations vs paid alternatives: VirtualBox has historically had weaker 3D acceleration, slower disk I/O, and fewer enterprise features than VMware Workstation. For casual use and CCNA labs it's excellent.

Exam focus: know VirtualBox = Oracle's free Type 2 hypervisor. Used for dev/test and learning.

children.name: Four network modes: NAT, bridged, host-only, internal

hack.memory: 'VirtualBox = Oracle's free Type 2.' Cross-platform, open source core, snapshots, shared folders. Perfect for CCNA labs with GNS3 or EVE-NG.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: install VirtualBox on your Mac. Download an Ubuntu ISO, create a new VM, install Ubuntu. Take a snapshot before major changes. Experiment with the four network modes — NAT for internet, bridged for peer on your LAN, host-only for isolated host↔VM, internal for VM↔VM only.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Low-moderate exam weight — VirtualBox is the go-to example of a free Type 2 hypervisor.

1.11.b.info:
VMware Workstation (Windows/Linux) and VMware Fusion (macOS) are VMware's commercial Type 2 hypervisors for desktop use. They share a common codebase with different host-OS integrations. Workstation/Fusion is the 'prosumer' tier of VMware's product line — above the free VMware Player but below ESXi/vSphere.

What you get for paying:

  • Better performance — historically faster than VirtualBox, especially for CPU- and I/O-intensive workloads.
  • Nested virtualization — run ESXi inside Workstation for lab work. Critical for network engineers studying VMware.
  • Advanced networking — more vSwitch options, custom NAT settings, finer VLAN control.
  • Direct ESXi integration — manage remote ESXi hosts from within Workstation.
  • Better 3D acceleration — DirectX 11/OpenGL 4.1 guest support for games and CAD.
  • Encryption, snapshots, cloning — all polished to enterprise standards.

Product segmentation:

  • VMware Workstation Player (free for non-commercial use) — basic Type 2 hypervisor.
  • VMware Workstation Pro (paid) — full feature set.
  • VMware Fusion (macOS only) — Workstation equivalent for Mac. Fusion Player free for non-commercial; Fusion Pro paid.

2024 update: after the Broadcom acquisition of VMware, the Workstation/Fusion product line has been restructured with changed pricing and licensing. Fusion Player and Workstation Player became free for personal use in 2024. Check current status.

Exam focus: know these are commercial Type 2 hypervisors from VMware, used for dev/test (Workstation = Windows/Linux, Fusion = macOS).

hack.memory: 'Workstation for PC, Fusion for Mac.' Commercial Type 2 hypervisors from VMware. Better than VirtualBox at performance and enterprise integration. Nested virt = run ESXi inside.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: download VMware Fusion Player (free for personal use on Mac) or Workstation Player (Windows/Linux). Create a Linux VM and compare boot time to the same VM in VirtualBox. Typically faster in VMware.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Low-moderate exam weight — know both names and their host OSes.

1.11.b.info:
Parallels Desktop is a commercial Type 2 hypervisor specifically for macOS. It's the most popular choice for Mac users who need to run Windows because it integrates deeply with macOS features (Touch Bar, Trackpad gestures, Retina display scaling, Coherence mode that blends Windows apps into the Mac desktop).

Key selling points:

  • Windows on Apple Silicon (M-series) — Parallels was the first hypervisor to properly support Windows 11 ARM on Apple Silicon Macs (M1/M2/M3/M4). This matters because Intel Macs are being phased out, and native x86 virtualization isn't possible on Apple Silicon.
  • Coherence mode — individual Windows apps appear as native Mac windows without the Windows desktop visible.
  • Automatic optimization — Parallels handles most settings for you (RAM, CPU, GPU allocation).
  • macOS guest support — you can run older macOS versions as guests on a newer macOS host (handy for testing compatibility).

Competitors on Mac:

  • VMware Fusion — more technical, better for engineers. Cheaper (free Player for personal use).
  • VirtualBox — free but weaker on Apple Silicon.
  • UTM — free, open-source, QEMU-based Mac frontend. Good for Linux; Windows ARM works but less polished than Parallels.

Exam focus: Parallels is the canonical 'Type 2 hypervisor for macOS' example, particularly notable for Windows-on-Mac workflows. Low-weight exam topic.

hack.memory: 'Parallels = polished Windows-on-Mac.' Type 2 hypervisor for macOS. Great for Apple Silicon + Windows 11 ARM. Paid but popular.
hack.practice: If you have a Mac, try Parallels' free trial. Install Windows 11 ARM and run Office. Compare the user experience (especially Coherence mode) to VirtualBox — the polish difference is obvious.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Low exam weight — know Parallels exists as a macOS Type 2 hypervisor.

1.11.b.info:
The Type 1 vs Type 2 comparison is the single most-tested virtualization concept on the CCNA. Know this table cold.

DimensionType 1 (Bare-Metal)Type 2 (Hosted)
Runs onDirectly on hardwareOn top of a host OS
ExamplesVMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVMVirtualBox, VMware Workstation/Fusion, Parallels
PerformanceNear-native (3-10% overhead)Slower (20-30% overhead)
InstallationDedicated install, requires reboot, owns hardwareRuns as app on existing OS
Use caseProduction data centersDev, test, labs, learning
DensityDozens to hundreds of VMs per hostA few VMs on a laptop/desktop
DependencyNo OS underneathDepends on host OS stability
ManagementCentral (vCenter, SCVMM, oVirt)Local UI on host

Key exam mappings:

  • 'Production data center' → Type 1
  • 'Runs directly on hardware' → Type 1
  • 'Engineer's laptop for labs' → Type 2
  • 'Runs on top of a host OS' → Type 2
  • 'VMware ESXi' → Type 1
  • 'VirtualBox / Workstation' → Type 2

The decision in practice: if you're building a data center, you use Type 1 (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM) on dedicated hardware. If you're a developer or engineer who needs to spin up a VM on your working computer without disrupting your OS, you use Type 2.

right.label: Type 2 (Hosted)
items: A few VMs on a laptop

hack.memory: 'Production = Type 1. Laptop = Type 2.' If it's in a data center rack serving customers, it's bare-metal (Type 1). If it's on your Mac running a Linux VM for practice, it's hosted (Type 2).
hack.practice: Make this flashcard and drill it until instant: 'Which type runs on hardware directly?' (1), 'Which type runs on an OS?' (2), 'Name three Type 1 hypervisors' (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM), 'Name three Type 2 hypervisors' (VirtualBox, Workstation, Parallels), 'Which is used in production?' (1), 'Which is used for labs?' (2).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Single most-tested virtualization concept — expect at least one direct Type 1 vs Type 2 question on the exam.

1.11.c.info:
A Virtual Machine (VM) is a complete, isolated computer implemented in software. Each VM contains its own full operating system (kernel, drivers, libraries, applications) running on virtualized hardware provided by the hypervisor. From the guest OS's perspective, it thinks it's running on a real physical computer — the hypervisor translates virtual hardware access into real hardware operations.

Virtual hardware components allocated to each VM:

  • vCPU (virtual CPU) — one or more logical CPU cores. The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical CPU cores, time-slicing as needed.
  • vRAM (virtual RAM) — memory allocated from the host's physical RAM pool.
  • vNIC (virtual NIC) — one or more network adapters, each with its own MAC address, connected to a virtual switch.
  • Virtual disk — stored on the host's physical storage as a file (.vmdk for VMware, .vhdx for Hyper-V, .qcow2 for KVM) or as raw storage on a SAN/NAS LUN.
  • Virtual BIOS/UEFI — emulated firmware the guest OS boots from.

Hardware-level isolation: VMs are isolated from each other at the CPU level using Intel VT-x / AMD-V. Each VM runs in its own memory address space enforced by hardware page tables. A crash, kernel panic, malware infection, or misconfiguration in one VM cannot directly affect other VMs on the same host. This is the strongest isolation model in virtualization — stronger than containers.

Portability: a VM is essentially a set of configuration files and virtual disk files. You can copy those files to another host and run the same VM there. VMware and Hyper-V use standardized formats (OVF — Open Virtualization Format) to share VMs across platforms.

Trade-off: strong isolation comes with resource weight. A typical Linux server VM needs 2-4 GB RAM and 10-40 GB disk just for the OS. Windows Server VMs typically need 4-8 GB RAM and 60+ GB disk. Containers are far lighter.

layers: App A | App B | App C

hack.memory: 'VM = a full computer in a file.' Own OS, own kernel, own vCPU/vRAM/vNIC/vDisk. Hardware-level isolation. Heavy (GBs of RAM, minutes to boot) but safest.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: in VirtualBox, create a Linux VM and allocate 2 vCPUs, 2 GB vRAM, and 20 GB virtual disk. Note how VirtualBox allocates them in the 'Settings > System' and 'Settings > Storage' panels. Install Linux and observe how the guest sees these resources as if they were physical.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Core exam: VM = isolated virtual computer with own OS; uses hypervisor resources.

1.11.c.info:
Server consolidation is the primary business driver for virtualization. Before VMs, each physical server typically ran one application for isolation and simplicity. This led to data centers full of underutilized servers — most running at 5-15% CPU utilization, burning power and cooling for minimal work.

Virtualization changes this math dramatically. A single physical server that previously ran one app at 10% utilization can now host 10-30 VMs at 70-80% combined utilization. The physical server goes from wasteful to efficient, and the total number of physical servers needed drops by an order of magnitude.

Concrete savings from consolidation:

  • Hardware cost — buy 1 server instead of 20. Fewer CPUs, less RAM, fewer disks, fewer cables.
  • Power & cooling — one server draws far less than twenty. Data center cooling scales with heat load.
  • Rack space — one host takes 1-2U vs twenty at 20-40U. Data center floor space is expensive.
  • Licensing — in some models, OS licenses can cover the host rather than each VM (depends on vendor).
  • Operational overhead — fewer physical devices to patch, replace, and monitor.

Beyond cost, consolidation enables agility:

  • Rapid provisioning — spin up a new VM in minutes from a template, versus weeks to procure and rack a physical server.
  • Elasticity — right-size VMs dynamically (add vCPUs, grow vRAM) as demand changes.
  • Cloning and templates — deploy identical VMs from a golden image.
  • Dev/test isolation — every developer can have their own VM without dedicated hardware.

Consolidation ratios: typical enterprise deployments see 10:1 to 30:1 VM-to-physical-host ratios. High-density VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure) deployments can reach 100:1.

hack.memory: 'Consolidation = stop buying servers for every app.' One big host with 20 VMs replaces 20 underused physical servers. Massive savings in hardware, power, cooling, space.
hack.practice: Mentally walk through this: imagine your company has 100 physical servers, each at 10% CPU utilization. Virtualize them onto 10 beefy hosts at 75% utilization. You just saved 90 servers' worth of hardware, power, and rack space. This is exactly why virtualization took over data centers in the 2000s.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Moderate exam weight — expect a question like 'what is the primary driver for server virtualization?' — answer: consolidation / better hardware utilization.

1.11.c.info:
VM isolation is the hypervisor's guarantee that events in one VM cannot affect other VMs on the same host. This is implemented at the hardware level using CPU virtualization extensions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) and memory protection (EPT/NPT page tables).

What VM isolation protects against:

  • Crashes / kernel panics — if Linux VM-A crashes, Windows VM-B and Linux VM-C keep running. The hypervisor cleans up VM-A's resources and restarts it (or alerts the admin).
  • Memory corruption — VM-A cannot read or write VM-B's memory. Hardware page tables enforce strict separation.
  • Malware — ransomware in VM-A cannot spread to VM-B's filesystem (unless they share networked storage or a vulnerable network service).
  • Runaway processes — a VM maxing out its vCPUs cannot starve other VMs; the hypervisor schedules fairly using resource pools and shares.
  • Privilege escalation — a root exploit inside VM-A doesn't grant access to VM-B or the hypervisor.

Limits of VM isolation:

  • Hypervisor escape vulnerabilities — rare but real (e.g., the 2015 VENOM bug, the 2019 L1TF/Foreshadow CPU bugs). These let a guest VM execute code on the hypervisor or other VMs. Patched quickly but the risk exists.
  • Shared resources — VMs share physical CPU cache, memory bandwidth, and storage. Side-channel attacks like Spectre and Meltdown exploit this.
  • Network isolation requires explicit vSwitch/VLAN configuration. Without it, VMs on the same vSwitch can talk directly to each other.

Comparison to containers: VMs have stronger isolation than containers because containers share the host kernel. A kernel vulnerability in a container runtime can affect all containers on the host; a hypervisor vulnerability is much rarer.

Exam focus: know that VM isolation is hardware-enforced and is stronger than container process-level isolation. This is why VMs are preferred for multi-tenant or high-security environments.

items: Crash in one VM ≠ crash in others

hack.memory: 'VM isolation = hardware-enforced walls.' Each VM gets its own memory space, enforced by the CPU page tables. A crash or hack in one VM stays in that VM. Stronger than containers.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: in VirtualBox or ESXi, intentionally crash a VM (force a kernel panic — 'echo c > /proc/sysrq-trigger' on Linux with sysrq enabled). Observe that the other VMs on the same host keep running normally. This is hardware isolation in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. High-yield exam topic — 'which provides stronger isolation, VM or container?' — answer: VM.

1.11.c.info:
Live migration (called vMotion in VMware, Live Migration in Hyper-V) moves a running VM from one physical host to another with zero downtime. The VM's memory state, CPU state, and open network connections are transferred to the destination host without disrupting the guest OS or any applications.

How it works (roughly):

  1. Pre-copy phase: hypervisor copies the VM's memory pages to the destination host while the VM is still running on the source.
  2. Dirty page tracking: pages that change after being copied are marked dirty and re-copied in iterative rounds.
  3. When the dirty page set is small enough, the source pauses the VM briefly (tens of milliseconds), copies the remaining state, and activates the VM on the destination.
  4. Network switches update their MAC tables via a RARP or gratuitous ARP — the VM's MAC now appears on the destination host's port.

Requirements:

  • Shared storage — both hosts must access the VM's virtual disk. Typically via SAN (Fibre Channel, iSCSI) or NAS (NFS, SMB).
  • Same CPU family (or EVC mode to mask CPU differences) — instruction set compatibility.
  • Fast network — typically 10 Gbps or better for the vMotion network. Large VMs (hundreds of GB RAM) transfer a lot of data.
  • Licensing — vMotion requires vCenter and a paid vSphere Standard or Enterprise license.

Use cases:

  • Hardware maintenance without outages — migrate all VMs off a host, do maintenance, migrate them back. Zero downtime for services.
  • Load balancing — DRS automatically migrates VMs to balance CPU/memory load across cluster hosts.
  • Fault tolerance prep — proactive migration when a host shows pre-failure indicators.

Exam focus: live migration is a key DC feature — know it exists, requires shared storage, and moves running VMs with zero downtime.

hack.memory: 'vMotion = VM teleport with zero downtime.' Moves running VMs between hosts. Requires shared storage + fast network. The magic is iterative pre-copy of memory so the final pause is < 1 second.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: watch a VMware vMotion demo video on YouTube. Even if you can't lab it (requires vCenter + shared storage), seeing the VM console stay responsive during migration is illuminating. For a real lab, nested ESXi + VSA simulated storage works.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Moderate exam weight — know that live migration moves running VMs without downtime.

1.11.c.info:
The VM vs container comparison is the second most-tested virtualization concept on the CCNA (after Type 1 vs Type 2). Know this table cold.

DimensionVirtual Machine (VM)Container
SizeGBs (full OS)MBs (just app + deps)
Boot timeMinutes (full OS boot)Seconds (just process start)
IsolationHardware-level (hypervisor)Process-level (kernel namespaces)
Guest OSEach VM has full own OSContainers share host kernel
Density per host20-50 VMsHundreds to thousands
Use caseStrong isolation, different OS familiesMicroservices, fast scaling, CI/CD
Example techVMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-VDocker, Podman, Kubernetes

Exam mappings:

  • 'Which provides stronger isolation?' → VM
  • 'Which is lighter/faster to deploy?' → Container
  • 'Which shares the host kernel?' → Container
  • 'Which has its own full OS?' → VM
  • 'Which is orchestrated by Kubernetes?' → Container
  • 'Which uses Intel VT-x / AMD-V?' → VM (hypervisor uses these)

In practice, modern deployments use both: VMs host the container hosts. You might have 10 VMware ESXi hosts, each running 5 large Linux VMs, each running 50 containers = 2500 containers on 10 physical hosts. VMs provide hardware isolation between tenants; containers provide fast deployment and scaling within each tenant.

hack.memory: 'Houses vs apartments.' VMs are houses — each has its own foundation (OS), plumbing (drivers), walls (hardware isolation). Containers are apartments — shared foundation (kernel), shared plumbing (system calls), separate living spaces. VMs = Heavy Houses, Containers = Light Apartments.
hack.practice: Drill this comparison table until you can recite it. Create a flashcard with 7 rows (size, boot time, isolation, OS, density, use case, tech) and two columns (VM, Container). Fill it in from memory. Redo until 100% correct.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Single most-tested container-vs-VM question. Expect at least one direct comparison question on the exam.

1.11.d.info:
A container is a lightweight, isolated process (or group of processes) that packages an application with its dependencies and runs on a shared host kernel. Unlike a VM, a container has no guest OS — it uses the host's kernel directly. The 'container' is just a bundle of the application binaries, libraries, and filesystem layout the app needs, plus isolation primitives that make it think it's running on its own dedicated machine.

What's in a container:

  • Application binary (e.g., nginx, Python script, Java JAR).
  • Application libraries and language runtimes it depends on.
  • Minimal filesystem layout — just what the app reads/writes, often based on a small Linux distro like Alpine (5 MB) or Ubuntu slim (60 MB).
  • Configuration files and environment variables.

What's NOT in a container (that IS in a VM):

  • No kernel — uses the host's.
  • No hardware drivers — kernel handles hardware.
  • No init system (unless deliberately added; usually a container runs just the app process).
  • No separate memory management — the host kernel's memory subsystem is used, with cgroups for limits.

Isolation primitives (Linux):

  • Namespaces isolate what a container sees: PID namespace (its own process tree), network namespace (its own network stack), mount namespace (its own filesystem root), user namespace (its own user IDs), UTS namespace (own hostname), IPC namespace.
  • cgroups (control groups) isolate what a container can use: CPU, memory, disk I/O, network bandwidth limits.
  • Capabilities drop Linux privileges the container doesn't need.
  • Seccomp / AppArmor / SELinux restrict system call access.

Result: a container is a fenced-off process group that looks like its own machine from inside, but from the host's perspective is just processes with restricted access.

Exam focus: container = packaged app + dependencies, shares host kernel, MB-sized, seconds to start, process-level isolation.

layers: App A | App B | App C (container processes)

hack.memory: 'Container = fenced process on shared kernel.' No guest OS. Just the app + deps in a filesystem bundle, plus Linux namespaces/cgroups to fence it off. MBs, seconds, process-level isolation.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: install Docker Desktop. Run 'docker run -it ubuntu bash' and notice you're inside an Ubuntu shell in under 2 seconds. Compare to booting an Ubuntu VM in VirtualBox — minutes. You're feeling the container vs VM speed difference directly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Core exam topic — 'what is a container?' expect the shared-kernel definition.

1.11.d.info:
Docker is the dominant container platform — the company and the technology that brought containers to mainstream adoption starting in 2013. Docker packages applications into container images (portable, versioned bundles) and runs them as containers (live instances of those images).

Docker components:

  • Docker Engine — the daemon (dockerd) that manages containers on a host, plus the docker CLI.
  • Container images — read-only templates built from layered filesystem diffs. Stored as tarballs.
  • Dockerfile — text file with instructions to build an image (FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD).
  • Docker Hub — public registry for sharing images. Also private registries like Artifactory, ECR, GCR, Harbor.
  • Docker Compose — YAML-based tool for defining multi-container applications (e.g., web + db + cache).
  • Docker Swarm — Docker's native orchestrator (less popular than Kubernetes).

Key workflow:

  1. Write a Dockerfile describing how to build an image.
  2. Run docker build -t myapp . to produce an image.
  3. Run docker run myapp to start a container from the image.
  4. Push the image to a registry with docker push for sharing.
  5. Anyone else can docker pull and run the identical image on their system.

Portability: this is Docker's superpower. A Docker image built on a developer's laptop runs identically in staging and production. 'Works on my machine' becomes 'works everywhere Docker runs.' This is a cornerstone of modern CI/CD.

Under the hood: Docker originally used its own runc-based runtime. Modern Docker is built on containerd (the container runtime) and runc (the low-level OCI-compliant executor). Kubernetes moved from Docker shim to containerd directly in 2022.

Exam focus: Docker is the canonical container platform. Know it for naming questions.

hack.memory: 'Docker = the container platform.' Dockerfile builds an image, image runs as a container, Hub stores images. 'Build once, run anywhere.'
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: install Docker Desktop. Run 'docker run hello-world' to verify. Then write a minimal Dockerfile (FROM nginx, COPY index.html ...), build it with 'docker build -t myweb .', run it with 'docker run -p 8080:80 myweb', and hit http://localhost:8080. You just built, ran, and exposed a container.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: Docker is the canonical container platform. Know Dockerfile, image, container, registry terms.

1.11.d.info:
Kubernetes (K8s) is the dominant container orchestration platform. Originally developed at Google (based on their internal Borg system) and open-sourced in 2014, Kubernetes manages containerized applications across clusters of hosts. It schedules containers, handles failures, scales them up and down, and provides networking and storage abstractions.

The name 'K8s' is shorthand — K, then 8 letters (ubernete), then s. Everyone in the industry says 'kubernetes' or 'kates' or 'kubes.'

Core concepts:

  • Pod — the smallest deployable unit. One or more containers that share networking and storage. Usually one container per pod.
  • Node — a worker machine (physical or VM) that runs pods.
  • Cluster — a set of nodes managed by a control plane.
  • Control plane — the brain: API server, scheduler, controller manager, etcd (distributed key-value store for cluster state).
  • Deployment — manages a set of identical pods, handles rolling updates and rollbacks.
  • Service — stable network endpoint (IP + DNS) fronting a set of pods. Load balances traffic.
  • Ingress — HTTP/HTTPS routing to services (think of it as a reverse proxy).
  • ConfigMap / Secret — configuration and sensitive data injected into pods.
  • Namespace — logical partition inside a cluster (not to be confused with Linux kernel namespaces).

What Kubernetes does for you:

  • Scheduling — decides which node runs each pod based on resource requests and constraints.
  • Self-healing — restarts failed containers, replaces failed nodes' pods.
  • Scaling — horizontal pod autoscaler adjusts replica count based on CPU/memory/custom metrics.
  • Service discovery — pods find each other via DNS.
  • Rolling updates — deploy new versions with zero downtime.
  • Load balancing — distribute traffic across pod replicas.

Networking: Kubernetes uses CNI (Container Network Interface) plugins for pod networking. Popular plugins: Calico, Cilium, Flannel, Weave. Each pod gets its own IP address; pods can talk to any other pod in the cluster.

Exam focus: K8s is the canonical orchestration platform. Know it manages containers at scale.

hack.memory: 'K8s = orchestrator for containers at scale.' Schedules pods onto nodes, self-heals, autoscales, rolling updates. Pods are groups of containers that share networking.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: install minikube or k3s for a local single-node K8s cluster. Apply a simple deployment YAML, watch 'kubectl get pods,' then scale it with 'kubectl scale --replicas=5.' You'll see the scheduler place pods on the node and the service load-balance traffic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: Kubernetes is the container orchestrator. You don't need to configure it, but know its role.

1.11.d.info:
Duplicate of the critical VM-vs-container comparison — this entry exists because it's tested from both sides (VM vs container AND container vs VM). Drill both angles.

Key differences (rapid-fire):

  • Weight — Containers MBs, VMs GBs.
  • Start time — Containers seconds, VMs minutes.
  • OS — Containers share host kernel, VMs have their own guest OS.
  • Isolation — Containers process-level (kernel namespaces + cgroups), VMs hardware-level (hypervisor + VT-x/AMD-V).
  • Density — Containers hundreds to thousands per host, VMs dozens.
  • Use case — Containers for microservices and fast scaling, VMs for strong isolation and running different OS families.

When to use which:

  • Pick containers when: deploying microservices, need fast scale-up/scale-down, CI/CD pipelines, same OS family across apps (all Linux), lightweight workloads.
  • Pick VMs when: need strong security isolation (multi-tenant hosting), need to run different OS families (mix of Linux and Windows workloads), running monolithic applications, legacy software that expects a full OS.

Combined approach (most common in practice): VMs host the container hosts. A VMware cluster runs Linux VMs that in turn run Docker containers orchestrated by Kubernetes. Each layer plays to its strengths: VMs provide hardware isolation between tenants/workloads, containers provide fast deployment and scaling.

Security note: a kernel vulnerability in the container host affects all containers on that host. A hypervisor vulnerability (rarer) affects all VMs. Therefore, containers are not recommended as security boundaries for untrusted workloads — run untrusted code in a VM, not a container (or use container sandboxes like gVisor, kata-containers).

hack.memory: 'Container for agility, VM for isolation.' When you need fast + light + shared OS, use containers. When you need hard isolation or different OSes, use VMs. Most modern systems use BOTH.
hack.practice: Run a Linux VM in VirtualBox AND a Linux container in Docker on your laptop. Start both at the same time. Docker will be ready in 2 seconds; VirtualBox VM in 45-60 seconds. That 20-30x speed difference is the container value proposition in one demo.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Near-guaranteed exam question. Drill the comparison table until you can recite it from memory.

1.11.d.info:
A container image is an immutable, portable snapshot of everything a containerized application needs to run: the application binary, its libraries, configuration files, and a minimal filesystem. Images are the artifacts that get built once, stored in a registry, and pulled/run on any container host.

Image structure:

  • Layers — images are built as stacked read-only filesystem layers. Each Dockerfile instruction (RUN, COPY, ADD) creates a new layer. Layers are deduplicated across images for space efficiency.
  • Base image — the bottom layer, usually a slim Linux distro (alpine, ubuntu:slim, debian:slim, python:3.12-slim).
  • Application layers — added on top of the base (your code, dependencies, config).
  • Metadata — environment variables, exposed ports, default command, entrypoint.

Image registries — where images are stored and distributed:

  • Docker Hub — the default public registry. Over 100,000 public images (nginx, postgres, python, node, etc.).
  • Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) — AWS's private registry, integrated with IAM.
  • Google GCR / Artifact Registry — Google Cloud's container registry.
  • Azure Container Registry (ACR) — Microsoft's offering.
  • GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) — GitHub's registry.
  • Harbor, Artifactory, Nexus — self-hosted/enterprise registry options.

Image tagging:

  • nginx:1.25.3 — specific version
  • nginx:latest — whatever is currently tagged 'latest' (moving target, avoid in production)
  • nginx:alpine — Alpine-based variant (smaller)
  • nginx@sha256:abc... — immutable digest reference

Image vs container: an image is the template. A container is a running instance of that template. You can run many containers from one image (scale horizontally by running more copies).

Exam focus: know that container images are immutable snapshots stored in registries, and that 'docker pull' + 'docker run' = deploy anywhere.

hack.memory: 'Image = template, Container = instance.' Build image once, run as many containers as you want. Images are stored in registries (Docker Hub, ECR, GCR). Layered filesystems deduplicate storage.
hack.practice: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53: run 'docker pull nginx:alpine' and 'docker image ls' to see the image. Then 'docker run -d nginx:alpine' and 'docker run -d nginx:alpine' again — two containers from the same image. 'docker ps' shows both running. Stop and remove them; the image remains. That's the image/container separation.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53, Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Moderate exam weight — know that images are immutable templates pulled from registries.
